item.title: 250 Years of "All Men Are Created Equal": The Improbable Story of the Declaration of Independence
item.body:
This weekend, the United States marked its Semiquincentennial — 250 years since the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776. Amid the fireworks and the America250 festivities, it's worth telling the stranger, richer story of the document itself: a text written in seventeen days by a 33-year-old, edited by committee, celebrated on the wrong date, and reinvented by every generation since.

## The wrong birthday

Here's the founding irony: American independence was actually declared on July 2, 1776, when Congress passed the Lee Resolution severing ties with Britain. John Adams wrote to his wife Abigail that July 2 would be "solemnized with pomp and parade... from one end of this continent to the other." He was right about the parades, wrong about the date. What happened on July 4 was the adoption of the explanatory document — Thomas Jefferson's declaration — and it was the document, not the vote, that captured the world's imagination.

## Written by committee, saved by editing

Congress assigned the drafting to a Committee of Five: Jefferson, Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Roger Sherman, and Robert Livingston. Jefferson, chosen partly because he was Virginian and partly because Adams considered himself too "obnoxious" to write it, composed the draft in a rented room at Seventh and Market Streets in Philadelphia. Then came the part every writer dreads: Congress cut roughly a quarter of his text, including a long passage condemning the slave trade — struck to keep Georgia and South Carolina on board. Jefferson sulked about the edits for decades. Most historians think the editing made it sharper.

And the famous painting of everyone signing on July 4? Fiction. Most delegates signed the engrossed parchment on August 2, and stragglers added names for months.

## A document that escaped its authors

The Declaration's second sentence — "We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal" — was written in a society that enslaved half a million people, by a man who enslaved hundreds himself. What makes the document historically extraordinary is how relentlessly later Americans weaponized its words against that contradiction. In 1848, the women at Seneca Falls rewrote it as the Declaration of Sentiments: "all men and women are created equal." In 1852, Frederick Douglass asked "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?" — an indictment delivered by holding the country to its own text. Lincoln at Gettysburg dated the nation's birth not to the Constitution but to 1776 and its proposition of equality. A century later, Martin Luther King Jr. called the Declaration a "promissory note" that America had yet to honor.

## Anniversaries as mirrors

Each 50-year milestone has reflected its own era. The 50th, in 1826, delivered history's most astonishing coincidence: Jefferson and Adams — collaborators turned rivals turned pen pals — both died on July 4, 1826, hours apart. The 1876 Centennial brought a great exposition in Philadelphia showcasing the telephone and industrial might. The 1926 Sesquicentennial arrived amid Jazz Age swagger; the 1976 Bicentennial answered Watergate-era malaise with tall ships in New York Harbor and a genuine, surprising wave of national affection.

Now comes 250. Like every anniversary before it, the Semiquincentennial is less about the parchment in the National Archives than about the argument it started — an argument over who is included in "all," which is to say, the argument that *is* American history. The document turns 250 this month. The promissory note remains open, which was always the point.
